title: Addiction and Bipolar Disorder
author: Caroline

seo.title: Bipolar Disorder and Addiction Counseling St Louis
seo.description: Plan Your Recovery in St Louis Call 314.467.8393 for Bipolar Disorder and Addiction Counseling in St Louis. Call 314.222.5830 for Buprenorphine/Suboxone ($99/visit)

body:
The highs and lows of a bipolar spectrum disorder can feel overwhelming. They cause the individual to become vulnerable to addiction and substance use.

What is bipolar disorder?

Bipolar disorders are marked by a mood cycle in which a person’s mood can be elevated or depressed. This mood cycle can look very different in different people.

Everyone has some sort of mood cycle. We all have good days and bad days. Individuals with a bipolar mood disorder experience moods that are more extreme and problematic.

Types of bipolar disorders

Bipolar I

 Formerly called manic depression, this disorder is now called Bipolar I. Bipolar I is the classic bipolar spectrum disorder that people are most familiar with. It’s marked by an episode of manic behavior that lasts at least a week, as well as an episode of significant depression.

Mania is marked by symptoms such as:

  • Grandiosity
  • Decreased need for sleep
  • Talkativeness
  • Racing thoughts
  • Distractibility
  • Increased goal directed behavior
  • Risk-taking behavior.

Sometimes people lose large amounts of money gambling while in mania. This is an example of a risk taking behavior.

The symptoms of mania typically cause major dysfunction in areas of life such as work or family.

The defining features of a depressive episode are:

  • Depressed mood
  • Sadness
  • Hopelessness
  • Decreased interest in activities
  • Weight change
  • Insomnia or hypersomnia (sleeping too little or sleeping too much)
  • Physical agitation or slowed physical movement, loss of energy
  • Feelings of guilt or worthlessness
  • Difficulty concentrating or making decisions
  • Frequent thoughts of death, sometimes including suicidal thoughts or urges.

Bipolar II

Some people with bipolar spectrum disorder don’t experience full mania. Instead, they undergo hypomania.

Hypomania is similar to mania and includes the same symptoms. The difference is that it is less severe. Hypomania often does not lead to the same amount of interference that mania does.

If a person experiences both a depressive episode and an episode of hypomania, they meet diagnostic criteria for bipolar II disorder.  However, they must never have experienced an episode of full mania.

People with bipolar II often are misdiagnosed as having depression. This is because they may not experience the same level of functional impairment as a person with bipolar I disorder. The distinction matters, as people with unipolar depression (no mania/hypomania) respond differently to medications than people who have bipolar I or bipolar II.

People with bipolar II sometimes don't know that they have a bipolar spectrum disorder. Bipolar II is often misdiagnosed as depression, but the specificity does matter.

Cyclothymic disorder

To make matters more confusing there’s a final type of bipolar disorder called cyclothymic disorder. A person with this disorder has both manic and depressive symptoms. Though these symptoms are present, they do not meet full criteria for a hypomanic episode or a depressive episode. This is because they don’t have three or more symptoms at a given time.

How do bipolar disorder and addiction overlap?

Bipolar disorder has one of the highest levels of co-occurrence with addiction. According to the National Comorbidity Study (NCS), people with bipolar disorder are 3.1 times more likely to develop nicotine dependence, 3.6 times more likely to develop an alcohol use disorder, and 5.1 times more likely to develop an illicit substance use disorder compared to persons without bipolar disorder.

Bipolar disorder’s interaction with substance use is twofold. During mania, a person is more likely to take risks and engage in substance use. When a person is depressed, they’re more likely to engage in substance use to try to feel better.

Individuals with a bipolar spectrum disorder are more likely to engage in substance use than individuals who do not have a bipolar spectrum disorder.

How can you treat both bipolar disorder and addiction?

When treating people with both bipolar disorder and addiction, our approach is to treat them simultaneously. A person with bipolar disorder may require a mood-stabilizing medication to help them make the difficult behavioral changes required for recovery. Once their mood has stabilized, we can start working on the chemical dependence on the substance. The medication can help give them more cognitive-emotional resources to help them address their substance use disorder.
